title: Subcontractor Pricing Guide & Trade Tracker Walkthrough
summary: Erin walked participants through two core playbooks: the Subcontractor Pricing Guide, a collaborative document used to organize all project specifications before sending out for trade pricing, and the Trade Tracker, a color-coded spreadsheet used to systematically follow up with all trades through a defined timeline. The session also included open Q&A covering pre-construction retainer fees, handling unresponsive subcontractors, managing multiple bids, coordinating trade walkthroughs, and structuring overhead and project management fees.

teachings.explanation: A pre-pricing document that compiles all room-by-room specifications—flooring, walls, ceilings, millwork, doors, tile, paint, etc.—so every trade bidding the project has a holistic, organized view of the home. It reduces phone calls, speeds up pricing, and minimizes errors from missing information.
teachings.quote: This is the reason we have this filled out, to just have people thinking about the home and getting their own answers without picking the phone up a bunch of times to us.

teachings.explanation: A spreadsheet that tracks every trade through a defined follow-up timeline (Day 1, 3, 5, 10) with each team member assigned a color code. The goal is to convert all cells to green, ensuring plans were sent, received, questions answered, quotes received, reviewed, approved, and budgets updated. It eliminates the 'I thought you were handling it' problem.
teachings.quote: These are the people involved in the tracking system. Because what happens is we want to avoid a situation where, hey, I thought you were doing this, or hey, I thought you were doing that.

teachings.explanation: By following the structured trade tracker process—sending plans on Day 1, confirming receipt by Day 3, addressing questions by Day 5, closing loops by Day 10, and receiving quotes around Day 11—the team targets completing full project pricing in approximately three weeks, extended to four for complex projects.
teachings.quote: We're going to talk about the process that we use to turn pricing around typically in about three weeks. That's our goal.

teachings.concept: Phone Calls Over Email in Construction
teachings.explanation: The language of the construction industry is verbal and personal, not email. Following up with trades by phone rather than email significantly increases accountability and speeds up response times.
teachings.quote: The language of construction is not email. We all know that. We can try to fight it all day long. But the deal is, it's a phone call in person, track you down.

teachings.concept: Pre-Construction Agreement to Avoid Being a Free Pricing Exercise
teachings.explanation: Builders should consider charging a pre-construction or retainer fee before investing significant time in pricing a project. This fee signals that the builder's time and process have value and filters out clients who are only shopping for numbers.
teachings.quote: If you're in an area where it's really competitive and you don't want to be just a number... 100% pre-construction agreement. It could be based on square footage. It could be a solid number. I've seen $100,000 if you want me to even take a peek at this.

teachings.concept: Diagnosing Subcontractor Problems as People or Process Issues
teachings.explanation: When a trusted subcontractor suddenly goes unresponsive or underperforms, the root cause is often a people problem (key staff quit) or a process problem. By asking directly which one it is, a builder can often find a workable solution rather than immediately severing the relationship.
teachings.quote: I'm like, listen, you either got a people problem or a process problem, which one? So you got to guide them.

teachings.explanation: When a subcontractor fails to deliver, the primary lens for every decision should be what is best for the project goals—not preserving the relationship, not avoiding discomfort. The relationship consideration is secondary.
teachings.quote: What is best for the project goals, period. Like, forget the pain points, what is best for the project goals.

teachings.concept: Overhead Recovery Through Cash Flow Modeling
teachings.explanation: Rather than charging ad hoc hourly fees, builders should model total monthly overhead burn, assign it as a percentage of project costs (e.g., 5–7%), and track whether incoming projects cover that overhead. This allows a data-driven, adjustable approach to recovering indirect costs.
teachings.quote: I'll look at our cash flow per month and our burn for all the overhead... if our overhead, when we're bidding jobs, let's just say we're assigning it to 6% of the project cost, I can see how many projects we have, and I can see how much we're either going under or over that number.

teachings.concept: Project Manager Fee Structured Per Month, Not Per Project
teachings.explanation: Charging project management fees on a per-month basis rather than a flat project fee protects the builder when projects run over schedule for reasons outside their control, such as city delays or weather.
teachings.quote: Maybe a project is 12 months, and now it turned into an 18-month project, and the project manager fee was set to one number, and now we've added six months to the project. Well, you just lost money on that project manager.

teachings.concept: Client Cost-Consciousness Scales with Net Worth
teachings.explanation: Clients investing $1.5–$3 million in a project are often investing a large portion of their net worth, making them more cost-conscious and stress-prone. Above $5–6 million, and especially above $10 million, clients tend to feel less financial strain, which changes how pricing conversations and boundaries should be structured.
teachings.quote: When we get to that $5, $6 million, it lightens. When you get to the $10 million up, typically their net worth is much larger, and so it's less of a strain, which is less of stress or burden.

actionable_advice: Fill out the Subcontractor Pricing Guide room by room with the architect, designer, and builder before sending out any pricing—hold multiple working sessions to complete it as a live, color-coded collaborative document.

Use the Trade Tracker to assign every trade a follow-up sequence: confirm plans sent Day 1, confirm receipt Day 3, check for questions Day 5, close the loop Day 10, and target receiving quotes by Day 11.
Color-code the Trade Tracker by team member so accountability is clear and no task falls through the cracks due to assumed ownership.
Follow up with trades by phone, not email—make calls on the designated days and do not leave the conversation until you have a confirmed yes.
When a reliable subcontractor goes unresponsive, call them directly and ask: 'You either have a people problem or a process problem—which one?' Then problem-solve from there before cutting ties.
Establish a pre-construction agreement or retainer fee before investing significant time pricing a project, especially for higher-end clients where your time and process have clear value.

For complex remodel projects, schedule one group site walk with all trades at the same time rather than bringing each trade separately—set the expectation that this is their one shot to assess the project.
Structure project management fees on a per-month basis so that schedule overruns caused by factors outside your control are automatically compensated.
Model overhead recovery as a percentage of project cost using a cash flow tool, and adjust the percentage up or down based on what you observe across your current project load.
Experiment with bringing the project manager into the last 10% of the pre-construction pricing process so they feel ownership of the budget before the project kicks off.
client_challenges: Renee asked how to handle charging retainer or pre-construction fees for the significant time invested in pricing and collaborating with design teams before a project is awarded.

Renee asked how to efficiently collect and update specifications from architects and designers, and whether to re-enter information from thick spec books into the Subcontractor Pricing Guide despite the risk of human error.
Renee asked about challenges getting subcontractors to adopt and follow the builder's systems and documentation standards.

Larry raised an active situation where a previously trusted subcontractor has become completely unresponsive after being awarded work, putting the project timeline at risk for his own parents as clients.
Renee asked how to minimize the number of site visit trips required by the project manager or estimator when coordinating multiple trade walkthroughs for remodel projects.

Janine asked how to recover increasing overhead costs—estimating, purchasing, and construction management staff time—through project pricing, including whether to set included hours with an overage rate.

Janine described a pre-construction process that stretched over a year with a client who continually requested additional pricing studies and scope changes, and asked how to better structure boundaries and fees to manage that.
A participant asked in the chat when project managers should be introduced into the process—during pricing, pre-construction, or only at groundbreaking.
notable_quotes: On the subcontractor pricing guide's purpose: 'This is the reason we have this filled out, to just have people thinking about the home and getting their own answers without picking the phone up a bunch of times to us.'

On the language of construction: 'The language of construction is not email. We all know that. We can try to fight it all day long. But the deal is, it's a phone call in person, track you down. It's a playground. It's recess. Like, that's just how it works.'
On diagnosing subcontractor problems: 'You either got a people problem or a process problem, which one? So you got to guide them.'
On project priority over relationships: 'What is best for the project goals, period. Like, forget the pain points, what is best for the project goals.'

On client cost-consciousness by project size: 'When we get to that $5, $6 million, it lightens. When you get to the $10 million up, typically their net worth is much larger, and so it's less of a strain, which is less of stress or burden.'

On training resistant subcontractors: 'Did they hate that we made them take the time to go through every single line item? They did not like that at all. But they're really grateful because they didn't lose $50,000.'
